Store brand entry has permanent performance effects that typically benefit retailers, consumers, and premium-brand manufacturers while harming second-tier brand manufacturers, leading to higher unit margins for retailers and premium brands, but not necessarily lower prices for all consumers.

Private branding can preempt brand specificity, motivating retailers to optimize their investment in product marketing only when they control the branding rights, thereby deflecting transaction costs associated with brand specificity.

The study finds that percentage retail margins on store brands are higher than on national brands, and that high store-brand share enables retailers to earn higher percentage margins on national brands, while heavy store-brand users contribute less to total dollar profit than light users.

The existence of multitier store brands is rationalized by their optimal quality levels, which, despite reducing product differentiation, do not decrease consumer welfare or channel profits, although they hurt manufacturer profits and make two-part tariffs ineffective in improving channel coordination.

The introduction of a store-brand generates notable changes in retailer demand and pricing behavior, increasing retailer margins for national brands while consumer preferences for national brands remain relatively unaffected, although price sensitivity increases in general, with heterogeneity in preferences for the store-brand observed.

Cross-category learning effects exist between different product categories for both store brands and national umbrella brands, with variations in the degree of learning across categories and differences in correlated learning between the two types of brands.

Manufacturer and retailer characteristics jointly determine trade promotion budgets for supermarket brands, with specific variables influencing the allocation of promotional funds across different trade promotion types.

Supermarkets owned by the same chain carry similar assortments within states, while across states, they version their assortment, influenced by ownership structure, clientele composition, and competitive presence, with regional brands distributed primarily by small chains or independent stores.

The success of store brands varies across consumer packaged goods categories and countries due to the influence of manufacturer and retailer factors, which can be categorized into global integration strategies, local adaptation strategies, or worldwide learning.

The store-within-a-store arrangement emerges as a strategy for retailers to balance channel efficiency and interbrand competition by allowing manufacturers autonomy over retail decisions, which can moderate interstore competition.

Brand is an important determinant of consumer choice, with heavily branded retailers holding a price advantage and consumers using brand as a proxy for retailer credibility in non-contractible aspects of the product and service bundle.

The national brand manufacturers (NBMs) respond to the threat of store brands (SBs) by either accommodating, displacing, or buffering, and their response is influenced by their ability to differentiate on quality dimensions and their cost advantage over SB suppliers.

Marketing conduct varies over the business cycle, contributing to the popularity of private labels, with private-label share behaving countercyclically and experiencing permanent gains during economic contractions. Retailers' support for their own labels during contractions and national brands' procyclical behavior in product introductions, advertising, and promotional pressure further influence private-label market share fluctuations.

The proposed multicategory model reveals that using restrictive functional forms of utilities or ignoring cross-category effects in incidence and quantity decisions leads to incorrect assessments on relative allocation of promotional expenditures across brands, and that retailers are better off contemporaneously promoting brands across the two categories than promoting them in different periods.

The introduction of economy and premium private labels affects the choice of mainstream-quality and premium-quality national brands, with economy private labels benefiting mainstream-quality national brands and the effects of premium private labels on premium-quality national brands being mixed.

The salience of brands at the point of purchase is determined by both in-store and out-of-store marketing activities, with approximately one-third of salience attributed to out-of-store marketing and two-thirds to in-store marketing, and the proposed methodology enables diagnostic analyses of visual differentiation levels of brands and SKUs.

A multinomial logit model of brand choice shows that brand loyalty, size loyalty, store promotion, regular shelf price, and promotional price cut significantly influence the share of purchases by brand-size, with different responses observed across brand-sizes.

National brand promotions and store brands attract different value-conscious consumers based on psychographic traits, leading to distinct consumer segments that manufacturers and retailers can target strategically.

A dominant retailer can strategically reduce its assortment by carrying only popular varieties, leading to higher assortment costs for rival retailers and relaxed price competition, while manufacturer power results in both product varieties being distributed through both retailers, facilitating retail segmentation and potentially lowering consumer surplus.

The study finds that branding hierarchy effects dominate those from product similarity, and SKU competition is more local than global, with only subsets of products competing within groups of comparable products.

The success of private labels is linked to economic cycles, with consumers more likely to switch to store brands during economic downturns and maintaining this preference even after recovery, indicating permanent effects on national brands.

The study finds that email and sometimes catalogs from a focal retailer-brand negatively influence other retailer-brands in the category, while paid search influences only the focal retailer-brand; however, competitor catalogs positively influence focal retailer-brand sales among omnichannel customers, and a sizeable group of customers who shop across multiple retailer-brands and both purchase channels are the most responsive to multimedia advertising.

The intervention increases the market share and preference for private label brands while decreasing the preference for national brands, indicating a significant informational barrier affecting consumer demand.

Both parties involved in conflict delistings experience sales losses, with the retailer being the more vulnerable party; vulnerability is moderated by manufacturer-brand and retailer-assortment characteristics, and long-term consequences show the retailer's market share recovering while the manufacturer's share increases.

Retailer display decisions can negate the equity of an established brand, influenced by consumer expectations about retail displays and the relationship among displayed brands, leading to reevaluation of high-equity brand valuations when an unfamiliar context brand disconfirms these expectations.

The impact of category management and retail competition on pass-through is influenced by brand and retailer differences, the nature of the cost change being passed through, and the interaction between demand-substitution and strategic-complementarity forces, which affects the cross-brand effect and the overall pass-through of costs.

Price promotions typically do not have permanent monetary effects for either manufacturers or retailers; they have a positive impact on manufacturer revenues but mixed effects on retailer revenues, with retailer category margins typically reduced by price promotions. Manufacturer revenue elasticities are higher for small-share brands, frequently promoted brands, and national brands in impulse product categories with low brand proliferation, while retailer revenue elasticities are higher for brands with frequent and shallow promotions in impulse products and low brand proliferation categories. Retailer margin elasticities are higher for small-share brands and brands with infrequent and shallow promotions.

The study finds that own-brand pass-through rates are, on average, more than 60% for 9 of 11 categories, and substantial evidence of cross-brand pass-through effects indicates that retail prices of competing products are adjusted in response to changes in wholesale prices, with larger brands receiving higher pass-through rates and small share brands facing disadvantages in pass-through from trade promotions.

The estimation of brand positioning maps using supermarket scanning data allows researchers to derive maps from observed choice behavior, study the direct effects of brand price and position on sales, obtain statistical measures of accuracy for estimated brand coordinates, and integrate cross-price elasticity with perceptual mapping, with preliminary findings indicating that brands from the same manufacturer are often located in proximity on the maps.

Price changes influence brand competition patterns, with higher-price brands stealing market share from both their tier and the tier below, while lower-price brands primarily take sales from their own tier and the tier below, consistent with a bimodal preference distribution.

The optimal market entry and OEM decisions of a firm are influenced by the horizontal and vertical differentiation of brands and products, leading to increased profits for both the entrant and incumbent when they agree on wholesale prices that mitigate conflict, and the OEM arrangement can nullify horizontal and vertical conflict under certain conditions.

Perceptions of how different a brand is from other brands in the product category affect perceptions of the brand's position within the category, with strongly discrepant brands resulting in a subtyped position and moderately discrepant brands resulting in a differentiated position.
National label producers can profit from being outsourced private label suppliers, and different private label supply arrangements have significant upstream and downstream impacts.

Competitive retailer prices account for less than 10% of the variation in retail prices over time, with pricing history, wholesale price, and brand demand being the main drivers of retail-price variation, and demand-based pricing and category-management considerations being associated with higher retailer margins, while dependence on pricing history and store traffic considerations imply lower retailer margins.

The revenue premium a brand generates compared with that of a private label product is a reliable measure of brand equity that reflects real changes in brand health over time and correlates well with other equity measures, advertising and promotion activity, price sensitivity, and perceived category risk.

The variation in national-brand purchase shares is significantly influenced by store-specific marketing mix factors, particularly prices and assortment shares, with market-level variation accounting for approximately 30% of the weekly purchase share variation across stores.

The study finds that promotional elasticities are higher for product categories with fewer brands, higher category penetration, shorter interpurchase times, and higher consumer propensity to stockpile, while there is no significant relationship between promotional elasticity and impulse buying or private label market share.

The manufacturer can influence the retailer's targeting strategy by increasing product differentiation, which leads to better alignment with the manufacturer's intended market segments, and if differentiation is not possible, the manufacturer will focus on higher-end segments, potentially neglecting lower-end segments.

given_idea: The unintended consequence of using slow-motion video in advertisements promoting sustainability is that it may undermine the perceived intentionality of the influence agent, leading to a decrease in product appeal, particularly among individuals with lower intentionality bias who are already under cognitive load, and thus may resist calls to adopt sustainable practices.
original: Slow motion video in advertisements can backfire by making the influence agent's behavior seem more intentional and extrinsically motivated, which undercuts product appeal, particularly for individuals with lower intentionality bias and under cognitive load, while the effect reverses when nonhuman influence agents are used.

TV advertising with COVID-19 narratives positively influences social distancing behavior, especially in the absence of government policy interventions, while government ads have a nonsignificant overall impact but show a significant negative effect in the presence of such interventions.

top_k.sentence: Slow motion video in advertisements can backfire by making the influence agent's behavior seem more intentional and extrinsically motivated, which undercuts product appeal, particularly for individuals with lower intentionality bias and under cognitive load, while the effect reverses when nonhuman influence agents are used.

TV commercials featuring slow-moving objects prompt high-level construal and consumer preferences for desirability advertising appeals, while fast-moving objects prompt low-level construal and preferences for feasibility advertising appeals.

The study examines the effects of advertisement characteristics on perceived ad intrusiveness and marketing outcomes, concluding that intrusive advertisements negatively affect attitudes and intentions toward both the advertised brand and the host Web site, while informative and humorous video advertisements are perceived as less intrusive.

The number of views of a video ad negatively impacts its persuasiveness, with an average decrease of 10% in persuasiveness for every one million views, although ads that generate significant comments or use humor may retain effectiveness.
Sadness-inducing videos lead to increased proenvironmental behaviors, but the effects diminish over time unless a nonbinding commitment is made immediately after viewing the ads.

Skippable video advertisements may be as effective as traditional video advertisements on a per-impression basis, and viewer preference for skippable advertisements is strong, suggesting that formats like TrueView can enhance the viewing experience without compromising advertising value.

Animation speed significantly affects arousal, memory, conation, and impression formation in web advertising, with both slow-paced and fast-paced animations eliciting different psychological responses.

This research explores the persuasive and emotional mechanisms through which creative ads influence consumer viewing and purchase intentions, hypothesizing that the level of involvement with the ad moderates the desire to postpone closure effects but not the emotional impact.

Dynamic retargeted ads are less effective than generic ads on average, but become effective when consumers show evolved product preferences, indicating that advertising content should vary according to consumers' decision-making stages.

The degree of animation in online banner ads has an inverted U-shaped relationship with memory and attitudes toward ads, where high levels of animation lead to negative cognitive and emotional responses, ultimately inhibiting ad recognition performance.

The bias in perceiving actions displayed in slow motion as lasting longer is influenced by participants' knowledge of video playback speed, which can be mitigated by providing explicit information about the video speed.

When consumers view advertisements with product images positioned congruently with their spatial representation of time, they have more favorable attitudes toward the product, and this effect is moderated by the need for structure and mediated by processing fluency.

Fast-forwarding through commercials alters viewer visual attention, with advertisements containing centrally located brand information positively affecting brand memory, brand attitude, behavioral intent, and actual choice behavior, even with significant reductions in frames and loss of audio.

High-imagery radio advertisements perform better than low-imagery ads on measures of advertising involvement, attitude toward the ad, brand attitude, and purchase intention, but suffer more when there is competition for cognitive resources, particularly when a visual-processing task is introduced. The detrimental effect of the visual-processing task is more pronounced for high-imagery ads than for low-imagery ads.

Averted gaze direction enhances narrative transportation and advertising effectiveness compared to direct gaze, particularly in emotional ads, while direct gaze can enhance effectiveness in informational ads and when viewers prefer not to identify with negative emotional content.

Consumers' eye movement patterns indicate that advertisement characteristics, such as color and boldness, significantly influence their information processing behavior and subsequent choice, with color ads being perceived and viewed longer than non-color ads.

The use of attention-getting advertising tactics can lead consumers to infer manipulative intent when there is an imbalance between personal investments, personal benefits, advertiser's investments, and advertiser's benefits, which in turn lowers advertising persuasion as measured by ad attitudes, brand attitudes, and purchase intentions.

The effectiveness of cause-related marketing ads is influenced by the type of visual presentation, product type, and gender differences, with product-oriented ads leading to longer fixation durations and females processing ads faster than males.

Native advertising may offer a solution to ad avoidance by presenting advertisements that resemble original content, leading to higher consumer engagement, but this engagement may also stem from consumers not realizing the content is paid advertising.

Slow motion replay systematically increases judgments of intent, leading viewers to perceive actions as more intentional due to the false impression that the actor had more time to premeditate before acting.

The digital video recorder (DVR) increases commercial avoidance among viewers but also facilitates the measurement of this avoidance, leading to more relevant and engaging advertising that reduces viewers' motivation to avoid ads.

TV ads can effectively influence viewer tuning and attention, with recreational product ads preserving audience engagement while prescription drug ads decrease it, particularly for ads related to more severe conditions; viewer attention is negatively affected by ad duration and placement, and ad content has minimal impact on attracting attention.

This study builds a comprehensive theoretical model explaining advertising avoidance on the Internet, finding that perceived goal impediment, perceived ad clutter, and prior negative experience significantly explain why people avoid advertising messages, with perceived goal impediment being the most significant antecedent.

The role of advertising likeability has changed over time, with commercials perceived as less likeable and less effective, while the predictive value of different advertising likeability dimensions remained stable, and the influence of advertising likeability varied for different types of products.

Need for cognition affects attitudes formed from advertisements, with high need for cognition individuals evaluating product attributes more than low need for cognition individuals, while low need for cognition individuals rely more on peripheral cues in ads.
The use of an anthropomorphic agent in Web advertisements increases social presence and telepresence, which in turn enhances advertising effectiveness measures.

Progression ads foster spontaneous simulation of the process through which change happens, making these ads more credible and persuasive than before/after ads, but this effect is moderated by impairing process simulation and high skepticism, and can backfire if consumers focus on achieving desired results quickly.

Cross-media consumption of Super Bowl advertising and Facebook content significantly influences ad likability, with television advertising contributing 60% and Facebook content contributing 40%; divided attention across screens negatively impacts viewer appraisals.

Exposure to subtle dynamic design elements in digital advertisements enhances consumer evaluations in rating tasks, with state-level arousal as the underlying process, while annoyance can create a negative effect on evaluations when dynamism is both arousing and annoying.

The study identifies three types of media interaction effects in cross-media advertising—synergistic, antagonistic, and asymmetric—and finds that 44% of media spend produces synergistic effects while 21% produces antagonistic effects, indicating that synergy is not the default outcome of cross-media advertising. Low-budget media significantly contribute to these interaction effects.

The study demonstrates a trade-off between advertising effectiveness and annoyance, revealing that while display advertising can positively influence purchase decisions, excessive exposure can lead to increased consumer annoyance, which varies based on the consumer's stage in the purchase funnel and is moderated by ad format and consumer demographics.

The influence of interactivity on advertising effectiveness may be a function of both the person and the situation, suggesting that interactivity can be both useful and detrimental in advertising under different conditions.

The integration of positive and negative affective stimuli influences overall affective responses to advertisements, content-specific beliefs, and overall ad evaluations, with recency and contrast effects observed on positive responses but not on negative responses.

Consumers' perceived level of distraction by background advertisements serves as a metacognitive cue that influences their evaluations of the advertised brand, contingent on the applicability of the distraction = interest lay theory to the context.

A cause-focused ad is more effective in hedonic product promotion, while a product-oriented ad is more effective in utilitarian product promotion, with other-benefit appeals facilitating the effects of cause-related ads for individuals with hedonic tendencies and self-related appeals enhancing the effects of product-oriented ads for those with utilitarian tendencies.

The spatial proximity between visual representations of cause and effect in an advertisement influences consumers' judgments of product effectiveness, with closer proximity leading to higher effectiveness judgments, and this reliance is weakened by consumer knowledge, nonmechanical causal priming, or expected time delays.

The proposed framework for estimating causal effects in online actions, particularly in display advertising through ad viewability as a natural experiment, significantly reduces bias in estimating campaign lift to less than 10%.

This research shows that memory-based choice benefits from advertising that enhances conceptually driven implicit memory whereas stimulus-based choice benefits from advertising that enhances perceptually driven implicit memory, suggesting that implicit memory measures may be more useful indicators of advertising effectiveness than explicit memory measures.

The study proposes that product-usage compliance is influenced by salience/mindfulness, consumer costs and benefits, advertising and distribution cues, and perceived threats of noncompliance, with significant heterogeneity in the effects of advertising across consumer segments.
The goal of the viewer significantly influences where and for how long they look at different parts of print advertisements, with the nature of the ad also playing a crucial role.

Matching ad format to a consumer's mode of information processing enhances advertising effectiveness, with comparative ads being more effective for analytical processing and noncomparative ads being more effective for imagery processing. When ad format is compatible with processing mode, it enhances information processability, making the message more persuasive and improving ad evaluations, brand evaluations, and purchase intentions.

A process-focus ad condition results in significantly higher behavioral intentions than an outcome-focus ad condition, and this benefit occurs when ad arguments are strong and credible, while inappropriate ad arguments lower behavioral intentions compared to outcome-focused thoughts.

The study demonstrates a chain of effects from the content of television ads, through emotional reactions and attitude toward the ad, to actual viewing behaviors, specifically channel switching and fast-forwarding through ads.

Visual imagery vividness operates as a latent cognitive construct in advertising, influencing consumers' attitudes and intentions, while individual differences in processing style moderate these effects.

The effectiveness of humor in advertising is influenced by the mechanisms employed, the intentional relatedness of humor to the ad or product, and the type of product advertised, with current practices favoring incongruity-based humor in humor-dominant contexts.

The study identifies three consumer segments that exhibit distinct patterns of visual attention to advertising, influenced by physical ad properties, product involvement, brand attitude, and advertising recall.

Repeated presentations of commercials can enhance persuasive effectiveness up to a certain point, but excessive presentations may decrease this effectiveness, potentially reversing the benefits of earlier advertising expenditures.

The most effective repetition strategy for advertising is a combination of spaced exposures that alternate between involving and less involving media, and this strategy is supported by the retrieval and reconstruction hypotheses regarding incidental and intentional processing.
The camera angle used to photograph products in ads affects viewers' product evaluations, with the effects likely emerging from specific psychological mechanisms.

Consumer online motivations (connection vs. consumption) lead to ad clicks on social media, which affect behavioral intentions, with perceived entertainment and informativeness values of ads mediating this relationship.

given_idea: Price discounts effectively increase demand for healthy choices among children aged 6-11 in a developing economy, with product selection, message design, target age, and repetition influencing the effectiveness of these promotions.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: The use of incentives, pledges, and competitions as interventions effectively motivates children to make more healthful food choices, with variations in effectiveness based on age group, particularly showing that younger children respond more favorably to competition interventions compared to pledges or incentives.

Supersized pricing strategies lead to increased purchase and consumption by decreasing the importance placed on health goals, but health cues can mitigate this effect, and supersized pricing can also increase size choice of healthy foods.

The majority of the effect of a price promotion is on brand choice (84%), with a smaller impact on purchase incidence (14%) and stockpiling (2%), and while the average magnitude of the effect appears to be smaller than first thought, there is significant evidence that promotions have a primary demand effect. Additionally, category-specific factors, brand-specific factors, and consumer demographics explain a significant amount of the variance in promotional response.

Changes in the frequency of price discounts affect brand choice decisions of consumers exhibiting variety-seeking and reinforcement behavior, with the effect depending on whether the brand offering discounts is a major or minor brand in the product category.

The dynamic effects of price promotions significantly influence consumer purchasing behavior, with treated customers being 22.4% more likely to buy promoted items compared to control customers, and the magnitude of these effects is 61% of the promotional effects induced by offering shallow versus deep discounts.

Increasing the price of unhealthy foods reduces their purchases without leading to substitution with healthy foods, while the interaction of income and price influences the substitution patterns between healthy and unhealthy foods.

Brief messages about health influence children's consumption of identical foods, leading to a preference for alternatives to foods described as unhealthy, which is consistent across different age groups.

The introduction of front-of-pack nutritional warning labels leads to higher prices for labeled cereals while prices for unlabeled products tend to drop or increase less, incentivizing price-sensitive consumers to choose healthier alternatives, particularly among lower-income segments.

The study finds that promotional discounts positively impact store traffic, particularly for high penetration and high frequency categories, while branded item promotions are more effective than unbranded ones; however, excessive discounting may lead to lower store margins, indicating that the costs of discounting a large proportion of items may not be justified by the profits generated.

Consumers' willingness to pay for a product after a promotion is higher when the product is offered for free compared to a low, discounted price, as the price serves as a natural anchor for value estimation, and a free offer does not devalue the product as much as a low, discounted price.

The effectiveness of various promotion frames at online stores differs based on base price and relative surcharge level, with sales price promotions being more effective for low-base price and low surcharge items, while free shipping is preferred for high surcharge or high base price items.

The frequency of price promotions and the depth of price discounts significantly impact consumer price expectations, which in turn influence consumer brand choice behavior, with asymmetric responses to promotion expectations.

Incentives significantly increase the fraction of children eating fruits or vegetables during lunch and reduce waste, with a larger effect observed in schools with a higher proportion of low-income children.

The sales promotion bump can be decomposed into three parts: cross-brand effects, cross-period effects, and category-expansion effects, each contributing about one third on average, with feature-supported price discounts strongly associated with cross-period effects and display-only supported price discounts having strong category-expansion effects.

Consumers exhibit asymmetric demand sensitivity to price changes for healthy and unhealthy food, with greater sensitivity for healthy food during price increases and for unhealthy food during price decreases, influenced by factors affecting impulsive purchase behavior.

Advertising counteracts brand loyalty tendencies toward repeat purchasing and reduces switching costs in the breakfast-cereals market, making it an effective option for stimulating demand compared to price discounts.

Discount pricing is an effective marketing technique that increases consumer willingness to purchase by framing a price as a discount from an earlier price, influenced by perceptions of quality and reference-dependence preferences, while the welfare effects of regulation to prevent fictitious pricing depend on consumer sophistication.

The study finds that weekly discount factors for consumers in storable goods markets are heterogeneous and average around 0.71, and that using a fixed discount factor leads to overpredictions of the effect of promotional depth on quantity sold.

Small price differences are highly effective in inducing substitution to lower calorie options, particularly for low-income households who are most at risk for obesity, suggesting that a selective taxation mechanism can serve as an effective health policy tool in controlling obesity.

The study proposes that demographic variables, particularly income and household size, significantly influence brand choice behavior and price sensitivity, with lower-income households showing a higher propensity to purchase private labels and larger households with higher incomes being more likely to respond to promotions for larger, more expensive items.
The model tests for the presence of reference effects of price and promotion on brand choice behavior, suggesting that promotional activity has significant reference effects on consumer response.

Price promotion lowers a consumer's motivation to exert mental effort, leading to purchase decisions guided more by affect than extensive information processing, which influences product choice and brand switching.

The ban on fast-food advertising targeting children in Quebec reduced fast-food consumption by US$88 million per year, primarily due to a 13% decrease in purchase propensity rather than a decrease in expenditures per week.

The sales response model reveals that immediate price elasticities, cumulative promotional price elasticity, and long-term regular price elasticity are influenced by brand-specific and category-specific characteristics, with significant moderating effects on price promotions, indicating that deep price discounts increase immediate price sensitivity, while regular price changes often have minimal immediate effects but can lead to long-term sales increases, particularly in categories with high price dispersion and frequent promotions.

The majority of purchase decisions from infomercials involve some degree of planning rather than being made on impulse, influenced by factors such as expert comments, demonstrations, previous product interest, pre-purchase thinking, prior advertisement exposure, the number of infomercials viewed, and having children aged 10 to 14 years old.

The tendency to make unhealthy choices is related to an individual's temporal discount rate, with high discount rates predicting unhealthy behaviors, and intention-incongruent actions often triggered by environmental cues rather than hyperbolic discounting.

The choice process of consumers in a market (variety-seeking or inertia) significantly influences the effectiveness of front-loaded versus rear-loaded promotional incentives, with front-loaded promotions yielding higher sales impact in both market types, while rear-loaded promotions may be more profitable in variety-seeking markets and front-loaded promotions in inertial markets.

Embedded premium promotions are more effective than equivalent price discounts, particularly for unknown brands, and can be optimized through strategies like customization and coverage, influenced by consumer affinity towards social causes, personal motivations, and demographic markers.

Nontraditional retailers offer lower food prices than traditional stores, with an expenditure-weighted average price discount of 7.5 percent, and the price discounts vary based on the market share of nontraditional stores in metro areas.

The increase in long-term exposure to promotions has reduced households' likelihood of making category purchases on subsequent shopping trips, while increasing the quantity purchased when they do buy, indicating a tendency to wait for better promotions, which negatively affects category profitability.

Marketing actions, such as pricing, can alter the efficacy of products, with consumers paying discounted prices deriving less benefit from the product compared to those paying regular prices, mediated by expectancies about efficacy.

A 2-phase labeling and choice architecture intervention improves sales of healthy food and beverages in a hospital cafeteria, with significant increases in green items and decreases in red items across both phases.

Consumers discount price discounts, and their purchase intention changes depend on the discount level, store image, and whether the product is a name brand or a store brand, with a threshold level for promotional discounts affecting purchase intentions differently for name brands and store brands.
The poor do not pay more for food; in fact, they pay less than average due to spending a greater share of their income on foods with quantity discounting.

The simplified nutrition scoring system positively influences consumer food choices by leading them to select higher-scoring products, while also reducing price sensitivity and increasing promotion sensitivity.

The combination of an unhealthy label and a price surcharge significantly reduces demand for unhealthy food, while the unhealthy label alone is effective for women but backfires for men, leading to increased orders of unhealthy food.

Consumers value food-away-from-home products more when they have nutritional labels, and their willingness to pay varies depending on the type of nutritional label, with reference prices leading to higher bids.

An increase in the magnitude of discounts increases the variability in category sales, while an increase in the frequency of discounts decreases variability; bulky categories and highly competitive categories exhibit lower variability in sales.

The use of reusable coffee cups can be significantly increased through a combination of environmental messaging, provision of alternatives, and a charge on disposable cups, while a discount on reusable cups does not have a positive effect.

The simplified nutrition scoring system positively influences consumers' food choices, making them switch to higher-scoring products, while also reducing price sensitivity and increasing promotion sensitivity.

Promotion-focused consumers search for alternatives at a more global level and have larger consideration sets than prevention-focused consumers, who search at a more local level and attach greater value to nonhierarchically structured options. Additionally, promotion-focused consumers value larger sets more than smaller sets, while prevention-focused consumers do not show a significant difference in value between set sizes.

The decision to use cents-off coupons is based on the tradeoff between the costs of using coupons and the savings obtained, and coupons serve as a price discrimination device providing lower prices to a specific segment of consumers, with users of coupons being more price elastic than nonusers, influenced by the opportunity cost of time and household resource variables.

A 50% discount on the price of a meal in a flat-rate pricing context leads customers to consume less food, suggesting that individuals aim to consume the amount that provides them with perceived value rather than maximizing consumption until marginal utility reaches zero.

Consumers prefer a bonus pack to a price discount for virtue foods, but prefer a price discount to a bonus pack for vice foods, due to the justification mechanisms associated with guilt and consumption.

The effects of marketing variables on household purchase decisions for packaged goods are sensitive to whether these effects are determined conditional or unconditional on a product category purchase, with variations in reservation prices and intrinsic brand preferences across households leading to biased estimates if not accounted for.

Cause marketing can significantly increase consumer purchases, and the effectiveness of cause marketing is moderated by price discounts, following an inverted U-shaped relationship where the impact is strongest with moderate discounts.

There is a significant, positive relationship between regular price purchases and promotion purchases, with shoppers visiting for promotions spending more on regular price merchandise than on promoted items.
Consumers become more price and promotion sensitive over time due to reduced advertising and increased promotions, indicating that responses to marketing mix variables change over a long period.

Changes in price endings and associated leftmost price digits influence consumer choice, with just-below pricing shifting choice share toward lower-priced alternatives, moderated by price level and shopping goals.

Consumer promotions lead to the acceleration of product purchases, which can manifest as either an increase in purchase quantity or a reduction in interpurchase time, with variations observed across different market segments and loyalty groups.

given_idea: By targeting social practices rather than individual behaviors, sustainability interventions can reduce consumer resistance and promote a sense of collective responsibility, which can be further enhanced by increasing psychological ownership and clear assignment of responsibility.
original: Consumers prefer flexible plans for themselves but rigid plans for others, influenced by emotional versus logical decision-making processes, and this preference can be altered to favor rigid plans through nudging techniques.

The word-of-machine effect indicates that utilitarian attributes lead to preference for AI recommenders over human recommenders, while hedonic attributes lead to resistance against AI recommenders, with this effect being influenced by the complexity of attributes, number of options, and transaction costs. This effect reverses for utilitarian goals when recommendations must match unique preferences and is eliminated in human-AI hybrid decision making. An intervention can attenuate this effect.

top_k.sentence: Consumer resistance to sustainability interventions emerges not primarily from unwillingness to change individual behavior, but from the targeting of individual behaviors rather than social practices, which leads to excessive responsibility, unsettled emotionality, and destabilized interconnected practices.

A practice perspective is a more effective approach for sustainability policy than focusing solely on individual behavior, choice, or technical innovation, as it emphasizes the importance of social practices in driving transitions towards sustainability.

The shift from self-interested consumption to citizen-consumer practices reflects a conceptual framework that emphasizes sustainability-oriented shared practices in naturalistic foodways, which help reduce the individual burden of sustainable practices.

Consumers are more inclined to engage in pro-environmental behaviors when the message or context leverages psychological factors such as social influence, habit formation, individual self, feelings and cognition, and tangibility.

Collective efficacy is significantly stronger when task difficulty is moderate rather than easy or difficult, and through specific collective and self-efficacy perceptions, sustainable intentions are gauged, even when controlling for attitudes and social norms.

The research demonstrates that environmental action is influenced by a variety of factors, including values, personal situations, and attitudes, and is structured around people's everyday lifestyles rather than a linear model of behavior.

The dominant paradigm of 'ABC' (attitude, behaviour, and choice) in environmental policy is limited and creates blind spots, suggesting the need for a broader understanding of social change that incorporates more relevant social theory.

This article posits that consumer responsibility for sustainable consumption (CRSC) is related to the sustainable consumption attitude-behaviour gap, and aims to explore a broader conceptualisation of CRSC to inspire further research and practice.

The article posits that consumption is influenced by the appropriation of items within specific practices, highlighting the routine, collective, and dynamic nature of consumption, as well as the emergence and evolution of practices.

This article argues that co-creation practices in collective consumption contexts are influenced by role rigidity, consumer heterogeneity conflict, participation access, and signposting, and that these practices shape coordination among consumers and facilitate social learning, ultimately optimizing value for all participants.

Psychological ownership for the individual (my planet) positively mediates the relationship between material values and pro-environmental behaviors, while psychological ownership for the collective (our planet) is positively related to recycling intentions and the intention to purchase green products.

This paper develops a practice theoretical account of collective action pertinent to processes of large scale social change, focusing on transitions towards sustainability, and identifies dispersed collective activity as a key contribution of practice theory that may give rise to collectives.

Individualistic, relational, and collectivistic orientations engender distinct patterns of relations with external and internal stakeholders and provide unique potential to advance certain forms of social value.

Ethical consumers are overwhelmingly high cultural capital consumers, and high cultural capital consumption strategies have shifted to incorporate environmental awareness and sustainability principles, resulting in an emerging high cultural capital "eco-habitus."

Social norms can act as both barriers and facilitators in addressing climate change, where well-implemented social norm strategies can motivate positive behavioral changes even in the presence of unsustainable norms.
Dispositionalism in attribution for behavior reflects a theory of social behavior that is more widespread in individualist cultures than in collectivist cultures.

The congruence of political ideology and persuasive appeals enhances sustainable behaviors, with message congruence mediating the relationship between moral foundations and intentions, and spillover effects increasing intentions for sustainable acquisition and consumption behaviors.

Commercial actors rework well-embedded self-monitoring practices to promote public health by constituting these practices as aesthetic, enjoyable, and shared, thereby addressing resistances to individualization and responsibilization in neoliberal health promotion.

The proposed multi-level theoretical model explains why organizations engage in corporate social responsibility (CSR) initiatives, influenced by various actors at individual, organizational, national, and transnational levels, driven by instrumental, relational, and moral motives, which can both facilitate and impede social change.

The study explores the dynamics of social practice, focusing on how elements, practices, and relations change over time, particularly in the context of climate change and behavior change, emphasizing the importance of configuration and coordination in practice-oriented policy making.

The study identifies five major clusters of compliance-gaining techniques, which can be categorized into socially acceptable and unacceptable techniques, supporting the conceptualization of these techniques based on their general dimensions and their relation to interpersonal power.

Focusing on individual-level solutions (i-frame) has misled behavioral public policy by neglecting systemic approaches (s-frame), resulting in modest intervention outcomes and deflecting attention from necessary systemic changes.
The online environment influences human behavior in various ways, creating a psychological environment that shapes interactions and opportunities for behavior modification.

The review synthesizes evidence that psychologically informed policies can reduce failures of self-control through self-deployed and other-deployed strategies, as well as situational and cognitive interventions.

Environmentalism is fluid and multifaceted, evolving over time to produce differing conceptualizations that become affiliated with particular groups of actors, and these label changes are not only symbolic markers but also performative, entrenching consequential regime transformations with regard to environmentalism.

Social cognitive theory highlights the necessity of personal, proxy, and collective agency in human development, adaptation, and change, emphasizing that these modes of action vary culturally and are essential for navigating diverse cultural contexts.

Changing social norms requires an understanding of how individuals perceive norms, influenced by individual behavior, group summary information, and institutional signals, which can lead to social change in behaviors such as recycling, intimate-partner violence, and peer harassment.
Dynamic norms can promote sustainable behavior, such as reducing meat consumption and laundry loads, even when they are counternormative, by motivating change despite static norms.

The Social Identity Model of Pro-Environmental Action (SIMPEA) proposes that ingroup identification, ingroup norms and goals, and collective efficacy determine environmental appraisals and both private and public sphere environmental action, driven by personal and collective emotions and motivations.

The innovative perspectives on socially shared behavior represent a new approach to the study of groups, emphasizing the importance of immediate interaction and reciprocal influence processes, while maintaining that individual-level processes are necessary but not sufficient for a social psychology of shared understanding.

Social theories of practice provide a new paradigm for understanding and responding to public health challenges, particularly in relation to smoking, by focusing on the co-evolution of practices and their material and symbolic elements.

Choice architecture interventions successfully promote behavior change across various behavioral domains, with a small to medium effect size, and their effectiveness varies significantly based on technique and domain, particularly excelling in food choices.
The characteristics of branded social media content affect different types of consumer engagement, with persuasive content characteristics lowering engagement in the social media context.

The congruence between individuals' notions of high-perceived control over shopping situations and participative price environments produces an implementation-oriented mind-set that influences cognitions and intentions, and in the absence of such congruence, implemental mind-sets can be created that replace deliberative thinking.

The neo-pagan movement reflects a matriarchal philosophy that emphasizes holistic and activist approaches to research, advocating for large-scale social change while respecting connectivity and observation in science.
Collaborative stakeholder engagement positively influences organizational legitimacy through shared goals and partnerships with various stakeholder groups.

The human tendency to act in an other-regarding fashion is more pervasive and important than recognized, driven by social context rather than personal payoffs, and this understanding can illuminate the nature of social norms and behaviors that solidify into norms.
Consumer behavior is not an independent discipline, is distinguished by its focus on the consumer role, and is not an interdisciplinary field.

The article discusses various perspectives on practices and their role in social orders, emphasizing the significance of collective action, tacit knowledge, and the implications of posthumanist challenges on social theory and agency.

The integrated theory-based intervention targeting both motivational and volitional phases effectively reduces alcohol consumption in excess of guideline limits among undergraduate students, with significant reductions observed particularly in participants receiving mental simulation and implementation intention strategies.

The study tested the hypothesis that mental imagery targeting key elements of implementation intentions further increases goal achievement, particularly among low fruit consumers, leading to higher fruit consumption compared to other groups.

The study finds that social perception may involve two subtle ways of dehumanizing others, as indicated by stronger congruent associations among social categories and traits compared to incongruent associations.
Social norms evolve based on individual psychological dispositions, which can either impair social efficiency or promote fairness norms that align with modern societal standards.

Printed normative messages can influence conservation behavior among hotel guests, with the alignment of injunctive and descriptive norms increasing their effectiveness, and both generic and specific reference groups impacting behavior.
The concept of 'practice' is central to social life, as explored through the evolution of practice theory and its historical context.

Mindfulness serves as an antidote to mindless consumption, positively influencing individual and collective well-being through the practice of mindful consumption, which enhances consumer, societal, and environmental well-being.

Corporate social responsibility initiatives can decrease consumers' intentions to buy a company's products under certain conditions, influenced by company-specific and individual-specific factors, as well as consumers' perceptions of congruence between their own characters and that of the company.

Organizational renewal hinges on the ability to link environmental change to corporate strategy and modify that linkage over time, rather than merely recognizing significant changes in the environment.

The choice of an eco-product influences downstream environmentally responsible behavior, with the effect being contingent on individual environmental consciousness, where less conscious consumers experience undermined behavior and highly conscious consumers experience reinforcement; this is driven by goal satiation and prosocial self-perceptions, respectively. Additionally, a point-of-purchase intervention can mitigate negative effects for less conscious consumers.

The consumption of products significantly influences the consumer's structuring of social reality, self-concept, and behavior, particularly when products serve as social stimuli that guide role performance in novel situations.

given_idea: Four major challenges in digital advertising have been identified: the need for more accurate ad performance metrics, the friction between ad channel partners, the rise of ad blocking, and the problem of ad fraud, all of which are unique to digital advertising.
original: This article identifies four market inefficiencies in digital advertising—ad effect measurement, frictions between and within advertising channel members, ad blocking, and ad fraud—that are poorly understood and manifest uniquely in digital ad markets.

top_k.sentence: This article identifies four market inefficiencies in digital advertising—ad effect measurement, frictions between and within advertising channel members, ad blocking, and ad fraud—that are poorly understood and manifest uniquely in digital ad markets.

The paper identifies the main reasons why internet users want to block online ads, evaluates existing ad-blocking techniques, and measures the bandwidth used by online advertisements relative to actual content.

The "ghost ads" methodology improves the measurement of online ad effectiveness by reducing experimentation costs, enhancing measurement precision, and demonstrating significant ad lift in website visits and purchases.

The study finds that at least 6.7% of websites in the Alexa Top-5K use anti-adblocking scripts, which are acquired from 12 distinct entities that have a direct interest in supporting the online advertising industry.
The study investigates the impact of exclusive ad placement on consumer behavior and advertising effectiveness in online environments.

The study finds that cross effects exist between online and offline advertising, with online display and search advertising being more effective than traditional advertising, primarily due to strong cross effects on the offline channel, and that traditional advertising decreases paid search click-through rates, reducing the net cross effect of traditional advertising.

Ad transparency impacts ad effectiveness by revealing unacceptable information flows, which reduces effectiveness due to increased consumer privacy concerns, while platform trust moderates this effect.

The study investigates the reliability of measuring advertising effectiveness and the causal effects of advertising on consumer behaviors, particularly purchases, using new metrics and methods in online advertising.

The study finds that user responses to sponsored-search advertising are influenced by the substitutability of ads, with substantial negative externalities from competition, and that optimal ad placements can significantly increase click-through rates and user welfare.

Matching ad format to a consumer's mode of information processing enhances advertising effectiveness, with comparative ads being more effective for analytical processing and noncomparative ads being more effective for imagery processing. When ad format is compatible with processing mode, it enhances information processability, making the message more persuasive and improving ad evaluations, brand evaluations, and purchase intentions.

The study identifies four main technical reasons for adblock software usage related to website technology and web development problems, including interruption, amount of ads, speed, and security, along with a social reason related to privacy concerns.

This article synthesizes findings from over 300 articles on online advertising, identifying key themes such as effectiveness, mechanisms, creative elements, context, personalization, and search advertising, while also highlighting knowledge gaps and future research questions.
The empirical evaluation of a metric tailored for auctions in online advertising correlates better with A/B test results than standard metrics.

The study aims to develop a comprehensive understanding of anti-adblockers and enable adblockers to bypass state-of-the-art anti-adblockers through differential execution analysis, which detects anti-adblockers on a significant percentage of websites, revealing that most anti-adblocker attempts do not result in visible reactions.

The use of multiple ad creatives in online advertising campaigns, tailored to an individual's ad impression history, significantly increases website visits and conversions, with observed increases of 12.7% and 13.8% respectively.
Each additional percentage point of site visitors blocking ads reduces website traffic by 0.67% over 35 months, indicating that ad blocking poses a threat to the ad-supported web.

The success of mobile advertising campaigns depends on understanding environmental, consumer, and technological context variables, focusing on advertising goals, accounting for market factors, and using appropriate mobile ad elements to improve outcome metrics.
Native advertising generates a higher click-through rate compared to display advertising, but display advertising leads to greater brand recognition and website trustworthiness.
The proposed model shows that pulsing advertising strategies can generate greater total awareness than continuous advertising when ad quality varies over time due to wearout and restoration effects.
The proposed ranking method globally balances the different goals of advertisers in the conversion funnel, leading to improved overall performance in online traffic at a major ad network.

The proposed methodology unifies ad buying, attribution, and experimentation into a computational model that significantly improves the return on investment of advertising by rigorously quantifying advertising incrementality.

Native advertising may offer a solution to ad avoidance by presenting advertisements that resemble original content, leading to higher consumer engagement, but this engagement may also stem from consumers not realizing the content is paid advertising.
Internet ads perform on par with television ads on brand-building metrics when accounting for preexisting brand knowledge, which is crucial for valid comparisons across advertising formats.

National, regional, and online advertising each have a positive and significant main effect on firm performance, but each advertising vehicle weakens the effectiveness of the respective other two advertising vehicles, necessitating strategic integration to maximize combined effectiveness.

Targeting technology impacts competition between local and general advertising outlets, restoring advantages to general outlets and increasing their demand for advertising, but its benefits are mitigated by constraints on advertising space and supply adjustments by outlets.
The practice of running annoying ads can cost publishers more money than it earns, indicating significant economic and cognitive costs associated with bothersome display advertisements.

The article explores the implications of advertising's functions on the measurements of advertising effectiveness, suggesting that a technique-oriented approach may not adequately capture these implications.

The skippable ad format leads to more advertisements and viewers on the platform, resulting in a strict Pareto improvement that raises the surplus of advertisers and the profit of the platform, as viewers use private information about the advertiser to make more efficient ad viewing decisions.
Copy testing is effective, no single measure suffices for all advertisements, certain measures are more suitable for specific objectives, and ads that are liked tend to outsell those that are not.

Disclosing to an audience that an ad was created by a consumer triggers skepticism about the competence of the ad creator and identification with the ad creator, affecting the effectiveness of advertising co-creation based on cognitive resources, source similarity, and brand loyalty.

Matching an ad to website content and increasing an ad's obtrusiveness independently increase purchase intent, but in combination, these strategies are ineffective, particularly for individuals with privacy concerns.

The study finds that dynamic ad formats have greater carry-over rates than static formats, but static formats can be effective for price ads and retargeting, with retargeted ads being effective only if they offer price incentives.

Exclusive advertising contracts benefit both advertisers and media outlets when commercial advertising is an important means for advertisers to inform consumers about their products, despite high levels of advertising potentially leading to fiercer product price competition and reduced profitability for both parties.
This paper aims to review, organize, and integrate the literature on online behavioral advertising and assess the state-of-the-art in order to facilitate future research.

Incentivized advertising leads to lower click-through rates but higher overall install rates, and it causes a temptation effect where users prefer immediate rewards over ad content, with stronger effects when rewards are delayed or of higher value, and it reduces ad annoyance, particularly on small-screen devices, ultimately increasing publisher revenue by $3.10 per 1,000 impressions.

The use of mobile ads in free apps leads to increased network data consumption, higher energy consumption, and necessitates repeated changes to ad-related code, which can negatively impact app ratings due to user complaints about these hidden costs.

Ad blocking has a positive impact on user engagement with the Web, as evidenced by significant increases in active time spent in the browser and the number of pages viewed among users who installed an ad blocker compared to a control group.

The decline in click-through rates for banner ads is due to surfers avoiding looking at them, suggesting that click-through rate is an ineffective measure of banner ad performance, and that advertisers should rely more on traditional brand equity measures such as brand awareness and advertising recall, which are influenced by repetition and the banner's message.

The preference for online advertising pricing models (CPM vs. CPC) is influenced by factors such as uncertainty in the decision environment, value of advertising, cost of mistargeting advertisements, and alignment of incentives under different market conditions.
This paper investigates how incentives to learn ad performance affect the advertisers' bidding strategies and the publisher's optimal mechanism.

This study builds a comprehensive theoretical model explaining advertising avoidance on the Internet, finding that perceived goal impediment, perceived ad clutter, and prior negative experience significantly explain why people avoid advertising messages, with perceived goal impediment being the most significant antecedent.

The digital video recorder (DVR) increases commercial avoidance among viewers but also facilitates the measurement of this avoidance, leading to more relevant and engaging advertising that reduces viewers' motivation to avoid ads.

Closed-loop equilibrium advertising strategies are more effective in explaining dynamic advertising competition than open-loop strategies, and competitive advertising levels may vary in relation to market share.

The optimal advertising for new products is determined by advertising effectiveness, discount rate, and the ratio of advertisement to profits, which can result in various shapes of advertising patterns depending on the interplay among these factors.

Different ad formats, such as display and search ads, affect consumer behavior differently at various stages of the decision process, with display ads having a significant early impact and search ads influencing all stages, and the likelihood of conversion increases when consumers interact with these ads. Additionally, attributing conversions based on the Hidden Markov Model provides different insights into ad effectiveness compared to traditional attribution methods.

The proposed framework for estimating causal effects in online actions, particularly in display advertising through ad viewability as a natural experiment, significantly reduces bias in estimating campaign lift to less than 10%.
There is considerable scope for improvement in the allocative efficiency of online display advertising markets despite their current high efficiency.
The iPinYou dataset is the first publicly available dataset on RTB display advertising, which supports experiments on bid optimization and CTR estimation in computational advertising research.

Dynamic retargeted ads are less effective than generic ads on average, but become effective when consumers show evolved product preferences, indicating that advertising content should vary according to consumers' decision-making stages.

The study proposes a statistically sound randomization-based methodology for measuring the causal effectiveness of digital advertising campaigns, addressing complications such as identifier instability and interference among observations that impair accurate lift measurements.

given_idea: The study finds a negative relationship between unemployment and drinking frequency, a positive relationship between unemployment and drinking intensity, and that total alcohol consumption is procyclical, with significant consumer differences based on income and age affecting drinking behaviors.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: Changes in the unemployment rate are positively related to changes in binge drinking, alcohol-involved driving, and alcohol abuse and/or dependence, indicating that problematic drinking may be an indirect consequence of an economic downturn.

Unemployment affects alcohol use and symptoms of dependence, with non-employment reducing both alcohol consumption and dependence symptoms, while involuntary unemployment has a mixed effect on alcohol consumption and dependence symptoms.

The demand for packaged alcohol is procyclical, with a 6.5 percent decrease in demand associated with changes in the state-level unemployment rate and personal per capita income during the business cycle, primarily due to a decrease in quantity rather than price.

During the Great Recession, there was a significant decline in overall alcohol use but an increase in frequent binge drinking, particularly among non-Black, unmarried men under 30 who recently became unemployed.

Job loss affects the incidence of clinically significant alcohol abuse, with greater incidence among those laid off compared to those not laid off, while employed individuals in low employment communities have a reduced risk of alcohol abuse.

Alcohol consumption has an inverse U-shaped impact on employment and wages for females, a positive but less pronounced impact on males, and no significant effect on employment when accounting for individual heterogeneity, while having a small, positive, linear impact on wage rates for both genders.
The prevalence of binge drinking is strongly countercyclical, with increases during economic downturns reflecting the influence of economic stress rather than just increased leisure availability.

The study finds that sobriety incentives significantly reduce daytime drinking among low-income workers in India, leading to a 50 percent increase in savings, without significantly affecting labor supply, productivity, or earnings.

Binge drinking is common among most strata of US adults, including those aged 26 years or older, and has increased per capita, particularly since 1995, with a strong association between binge drinking and alcohol-impaired driving.

The study suggests that economic conditions have a significant relationship with health, indicating that total mortality and most causes of death exhibit procyclical variation, with the exception of suicides, and that the impact of personal incomes on health is weak and sensitive to various factors.

The study finds that dropping out of high school has no effect on alcohol-related problems for individuals in their mid-twenties but is associated with significantly increased levels of alcohol-related problems for individuals in their mid-thirties, while going on to college is associated with lower levels of heavy drinking in late twenties and thirties, and strong gender and ethnicity effects diminish with age.
The results from both studies support both drift and social causation processes and demonstrate that at least one diagnosis-specific psychopathology is linked to unemployment.

The quantity-frequency (QF) method and the diary method yield different results in measuring alcohol consumption, with the diary method reporting higher consumption at lower levels and a linear relationship observed between the two methods, suggesting careful selection of measures is necessary for research purposes.

Depression mediates the relationship between financial strain and drinking to cope, and drinking to cope mediates the relationship between depression and alcohol use and abuse, with gender and race moderating several relationships in the model.

Increases in working hours are associated with an increase in cigarette smoking, a reduction in physical activity, and fewer visits to physicians, while increases in wages are associated with greater consumption of cigarettes.

The study supports the hypothesis that entry wages are more procyclical than wages of stayers, with a one point increase in the unemployment rate decreasing wages of newly hired workers by around 2.7 percent and by 2.2 percent for stayers within the same firm-job title.

A one percentage point increase in the state unemployment rate is associated with a 1.67 percentage point reduction in the probability that men have health insurance, with this effect being strongest among college-educated, white, and older men, while for women and children, health insurance coverage is not significantly correlated with the unemployment rate.

Spending drops sharply at the exhaustion of unemployment insurance benefits, which is inconsistent with rational models of liquidity-constrained households but consistent with behavioral models, indicating that the consumption-smoothing gains from extending UI benefits are significantly larger than from raising UI benefit levels.

The measures of alcohol consumption used in the household survey demonstrate high levels of reliability, averaging .91 for consumption measures of beer, wine, and distilled spirits, indicating that these measures can be used with considerable confidence.

The study suggests that income inequality and various nonclinical factors, such as education and employment, significantly influence health outcomes, with a particular emphasis on the role of income as a social determinant of health.
The impact of structural factors on the relationship between unemployment and health has been largely ignored, and an historical perspective can provide useful insights into this issue.

Consumers underreact to non-salient taxes, as evidenced by a field experiment showing that tax-inclusive pricing reduces demand by 8 percent, and that increases in taxes included in posted prices reduce alcohol consumption more than those applied at the register. The economic incidence of a tax is influenced by its statutory incidence, leading to potential efficiency losses even without behavioral changes.
Alcohol and marijuana are economic complements, and policies that increase the full price of alcohol decrease participation in marijuana use.

The implementation of the alcoholic beverage warning label has led to a statistically significant decrease in alcohol consumption among lighter drinkers during pregnancy, while risk drinkers did not show a significant change in their alcohol consumption.

The overall 12-month prevalence of addiction among U.S. adults varies from 15% to 61%, with the most plausible estimate being that 47% of the population suffers from maladaptive signs of an addictive disorder, indicating that addictions may arise from lifestyle problems as well as person-level factors.

Sensitivity to reward mediates social drinkers' reactivity to alcohol cues, with heavy drinkers showing increased responsivity to rewards and urge to drink after alcohol exposure, and heightened sensitivity to reward being significantly related to cue-elicited urge to drink and positive affect.

The dynamic model of rational addiction shows that consumers' responsiveness to cigarette taxes is altered due to addiction, leading to a higher category elasticity and potential substitution effects that can increase overall consumption for some consumers.

The protective effect of alcohol consumption on the risk of myocardial infarction is dependent on the pattern, frequency, type, and intensity of alcohol consumed, while alcohol also modifies the effects of smoking on the lipid profile and inflammation markers. Regular drinking attenuates the effect of smoking on hs-CRP and lipid profile.

The model shows that a significant portion of consumers self-regulate their beverage consumption by balancing short-run needs with long-term goals, and that a product satisfying a combination of "health-hydrating" needs can expand overall beverage consumption by as much as 5%.

Consumer pessimism about unemployment explains a substantial part of the recent weakness in consumption, and the buffer-stock model of saving implies a central role for unemployment expectations in determining current consumption.

The impacts of the Great Recession on labor market outcomes are not uniform across demographic groups, with the strongest effects felt by men, black and Hispanic workers, youth, and low-education workers, largely explained by differences in exposure to cycles across industry-occupation employment.
The consumption response is sensitive to income changes, with different empirical approaches revealing varying degrees of marginal propensity to consume out of income shocks.

Taxation of distilled spirits can lead to increased tax revenue and improved consumer welfare while maintaining fixed alcohol consumption, though it may be slightly regressive compared to market structure regulations like post and hold.

The theory of rational addiction suggests that strong addiction is characterized by a significant effect of past consumption on current consumption, leading to unstable steady states and influencing behaviors such as bingeing and responses to price changes.
Workplace smoking bans reduce smoking prevalence by 5 percentage points and average daily consumption among smokers by 10 percent, with the greatest impact observed for those with longer work weeks.

The study proposes that the Rosenbaum bounds approach and instrumental variables estimation provide complementary information about causal relationships, particularly in estimating the effects of unemployment insurance on reemployment timing, postunemployment wage, and relocation probability, while addressing bias from unobserved variables.
Coffee-related genetic risk scores are positively associated with coffee and tea consumption, but their association with alcohol consumption requires further investigation to determine causality.

Cigarette taxes are less than fully passed through to consumer prices, indicating that consumers and producers share the excess burden of these taxes, with significant variations in incidence based on household income and education levels, and tax avoidance opportunities affecting purchasing behavior.

Households with low permanent incomes have subjective rates of time preference that are three to five percentage points higher than those of households with high permanent incomes, and controlling for race and education widens this difference, suggesting implications for consumption patterns and savings behavior across socioeconomic classes.

The increase in deaths of despair among the working class in America is linked to social and economic factors, including the decline of family, community, and religion, as well as stagnating wages and job quality, leading to a disturbing decline in life expectancy.

Consumption inequality has closely tracked income inequality over the period 1980-2007, with a significantly greater increase in consumption inequality than what is obtained from total household expenditure data.

Households in less healthy counties consume more regular soda per capita, have stronger preferences, and are less price sensitive than households in more healthy counties, suggesting that soda tax proposals should be county-specific, and that most of a counterfactual tax-induced shift in consumption is due to an income effect and low cross-price elasticities.

Childhood socioeconomic status influences desire for health coverage in adulthood, with those who grew up poor generally less interested in health coverage compared to those who grew up wealthy, and this effect reverses when provided with base-rate information about disease.

This paper examines the response of household consumption to predictable income tax refunds, testing the life-cycle theory which suggests that consumption should not increase upon receipt of a refund, and provides estimates of the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) out of refunds.

Caffeine intakes among US children and adults have declined from 175 mg/day in 1999-2000 to 142 mg/day in 2011-2012, largely due to a decrease in caffeine consumption from soda, while store-bought coffee and tea remain the principal sources of caffeine intake.

Changes in income influence dietary patterns, food intake, and food preferences, leading to alterations in food choices relevant to health promotion, although further study is needed to explore long-term results.
